preFix.title: Recognising refrigerant problems in an air-source heat pump
preFix.subtitle: What to look for when COP falls, the outdoor unit ices up, or the compressor short-cycles
preFix.excerpt: A heat pump that has lost refrigerant charge will run longer for less output, ice the outdoor coil in milder weather than normal, and show a significantly reduced coefficient of performance. Refrigerant handling requires an F-gas certificate and must be carried out by a registered engineer. This guide covers the diagnostic signs so you can identify the problem before calling a service engineer and give them useful information on arrival.
preFix.sourceNotes: MCS (Microgeneration Certification Scheme) heat pump maintenance guidance. Environment Agency F-gas regulations (SI 2015/310, amended 2022). Heat Pump Association technical bulletin on refrigerant leak detection (2023).

content.text: circuit in a heat pump is a sealed system. It does not consume refrigerant in normal operation; a low charge after installation indicates a leak. Under F-gas regulations, only a technician with an F-gas certificate may handle refrigerants. You can identify the symptoms yourself and give the service engineer useful information before the visit.
Reduced coefficient of performance. A monitoring app showing COP below 2.0 in mild weather. When it previously showed 2.8 to 3.5, is the clearest early indicator of low refrigerant charge.

Ice on the outdoor coil in weather above 2 degrees C. A small amount of frost is normal; the defrost cycle clears it. Ice that does not clear after a 10 to 15 minute defrost. Or frost forming above 5 degrees C, suggests low refrigerant charge or airflow blockage.
Short cycling: the compressor starting and stopping every 2 to 5 minutes instead of running for 20 to 40 minutes. This can indicate high-pressure cutout from low charge or a blocked filter.

Hissing or bubbling sounds from the outdoor unit or internal connections: refrigerant leaking from a connection can produce a hissing noise. Do not attempt to inspect the refrigerant connections yourself.

If you observe two or more of the above signs. Book a service visit with an MCS-registered heat pump engineer holding an F-gas certificate. Before the visit, note the outdoor ambient temperature at the time of the symptom, the flow temperature setting. And the COP reading from the monitoring app. An F-gas leak check and re-charge costs 200 to 500 pounds depending on the refrigerant type and quantity required.

The following are not faults: steam from the outdoor unit during defrost. The fan stopping when the compressor is off, louder noise in cold weather. And a small pool of water under the unit during defrost. All are normal for an air-source heat pump in UK conditions.

attrs.symptom: Outdoor unit is completely iced over and not defrosting

content.text: Do not attempt to de-ice manually with hot water or a tool: this risks damaging the aluminium fins. Turn the heat pump off and allow it to defrost naturally. Once defrosted, check that the outdoor unit air intake is not blocked by leaves, snow, or garden furniture. If icing returns within 24 hours, call a service engineer.

attrs.symptom: Heat pump runs continuously but house is not reaching set temperature

content.text: In very cold weather. An air-source heat pump designed for UK climate may not deliver full rated output and a backup electric element may need to assist. Check that the backup immersion or supplementary heater is enabled in the settings. If the flow temperature is reaching the set point but rooms are still cold. The issue is heat distribution rather than the heat pump itself.
Step-by-step instructions for Recognising refrigerant problems in an air-source heat pump go here.
